schools.summary: Tola of Issachar arising to defend Israel after Abimelech: 'and after Abimelech there arose to defend Israel Tola the son of Puah, the son of Dodo, a man of Issachar; and he dwelt in Shamir in mount Ephraim' — Tola of Issachar rose to save Israel after Abimelech's death (the and after Abimelech there arose to defend Israel Tola the son of Puah, the son of Dodo, a man of Issachar, Tola arose, the and he dwelt in Shamir in mount Ephraim, his seat, the figure, the quiet deliverer raised up after the usurper, God's mercy renewed, the truth that God raised Tola to defend Israel after the ruin of Abimelech, Judges 2:16, the figure of God's mercy in raising deliverers, the peaceful judge after the tyrant, the rise of Tola, the judge raised, vv.16); Tola raised to defend Israel (the deliverer, Judges 2:16) — the deliverer (Tola); 'he dwelt in Shamir in mount Ephraim' (Tola's seat at Shamir, the judge's place, the figure, the peaceful judge after the tyrant, the providence, the rise, Tola judging twenty-three years to follow, vv.2) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Israel's deep apostasy, serving the gods of all the surrounding nations and forsaking the LORD: 'and the children of Israel did evil again in the sight of the LORD, and served Baalim, and Ashtaroth, and the gods of Syria, and the gods of Zidon, and the gods of Moab, and the gods of the children of Ammon, and the gods of the Philistines, and forsook the LORD, and served not him' — the deep and manifold apostasy — 'and the children of Israel did evil again in the sight of the LORD, and served Baalim, and Ashtaroth, and the gods of Syria, and the gods of Zidon, and the gods of Moab, and the gods of the children of Ammon, and the gods of the Philistines, and forsook the LORD, and served not him': Israel sank to a deeper idolatry than ever, serving the gods of all the nations round about (the witnesses noting the significance — the fullest and basest apostasy recorded in the book, Israel no longer serving one or two false gods but the gods of seven nations, the whole pantheon of the heathen, a heart so given over to idolatry that it embraced every false god and utterly forsook the true, the seven-fold idolatry the measure of Israel's unfaithfulness, the deeper they sank the wider their idolatry spread, the more they forsook the LORD the more gods they served, the lesson — that the heart which leaves the one true God does not stop at one idol but multiplies them, the apostate appetite knowing no bounds, 'my people have committed two evils; they have forsaken me the fountain of living waters, and hewed them out cisterns,' Jeremiah 2:13, the truth that to forsake God is to be enslaved to a multitude of false masters, the soul that will not serve the one Lord becoming the servant of many lords, 'they that go after another god shall multiply their sorrows,' Psalm 16:4, the figure — the bottomless descent of idolatry once the true God is forsaken, fulfilled in every heart that, refusing God, gives itself to a thousand idols, 'even as they did not like to retain God in their knowledge,' Romans 1:28, the truth that the depth of the fall is the measure of the mercy that will yet recover them, the figure of the heart that, having left the one true God, plunges into every idolatry without restraint, the call to cleave to the one Lord lest we be enslaved to many, the warning that forsaking God opens the door to every false worship, the figure of the heart that, having left the one true God, plunges into every idolatry without restraint); Israel serving the gods of seven nations, a deeper and wider idolatry than ever, the heart given over to every false god (the manifold apostasy, Jeremiah 2:13) — the idolatry (the gods of the nations); 'and forsook the LORD, and served not him' (Israel utterly forsaking the LORD and serving him not at all, the soul that leaves the one Lord enslaved to many, multiplying its sorrows, the forsaking of God, Psalm 16:4; Romans 1:28) — the figure (the bottomless descent of idolatry once the true God is forsaken, the providence, the apostasy, God selling them to the Philistines and Ammon to follow, vv.7) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Israel's seven-fold idolatry signify?

sides.position: The heart that, having left the one true God, plunges into every idolatry without restraint - the fullest apostasy in the book, serving the gods of seven nations and forsaking the true, the heart that leaves God multiplying idols without bound (Jeremiah 2:13), the soul that will not serve one Lord enslaved to many (Psalm 16:4), the bottomless descent of idolatry once God is forsaken (Romans 1:28)

schools.summary: Israel crying to the LORD and confessing their sin of forsaking him for Baal: 'and the children of Israel cried unto the LORD, saying, We have sinned against thee, both because we have forsaken our God, and also served Baalim' — Israel cried out and confessed their double sin of forsaking God and serving Baal (the and the children of Israel cried unto the LORD, saying, We have sinned against thee, the confession, the both because we have forsaken our God, and also served Baalim, the sin owned, the figure, the cry of the afflicted, the confession wrung out by the scourge, the truth that Israel, driven by distress, confessed their forsaking of God and serving of Baal, Psalm 78:34, the figure of the affliction that brings the sinner to confession, the cry of the chastened, the cry of Israel, the confession of sin, vv.9); Israel crying and confessing their sin (the confession, Psalm 78:34) — the cry (we have sinned); 'we have forsaken our God, and also served Baalim' (the double sin confessed, the forsaking and the serving, the figure, the affliction that brings the sinner to confession, the providence, the confession, God's rehearsal of his past deliverances to follow, vv.11-12) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: God rehearsing his past deliverances of Israel: 'and the LORD said unto the children of Israel, Did not I deliver you from the Egyptians, and from the Amorites, from the children of Ammon, and from the Philistines?' — God recalled the many times he had delivered Israel (the and the LORD said unto the children of Israel, Did not I deliver you from the Egyptians, and from the Amorites, the past deliverances, the from the children of Ammon, and from the Philistines?, and from many foes, the figure, the rehearsal of God's mercies to convict the ungrateful, the long record of deliverance, the truth that God recalled his many deliverances to shame Israel's apostasy, Psalm 106:43, the figure of the God who pleads his past mercies against the ingratitude of his people, the rehearsal of deliverances, the mercies recalled, vv.10); God recalling his deliverances from Egypt and the Amorites (the rehearsal, Psalm 106:43) — the deliverances (from Egypt); 'from the children of Ammon, and from the Philistines?' (the many deliverances from many foes, the record, the figure, the God who pleads his past mercies against ingratitude, the providence, the rehearsal, the continuation of the record to follow, vv.12) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: God continuing the record of deliverances, when Israel cried he saved them: 'the Zidonians also, and the Amalekites, and the Maonites, did oppress you; and ye cried to me, and I delivered you out of their hand' — God recalled more oppressors from whom he had delivered Israel at their cry (the the Zidonians also, and the Amalekites, and the Maonites, did oppress you, more oppressors, the and ye cried to me, and I delivered you out of their hand, and I delivered you, the figure, the long faithfulness of God to deliver at every cry, the mercy that answered always, the truth that whenever Israel cried God delivered them, his mercy unfailing, Nehemiah 9:28, the figure of the God who has always answered the cry of his people, the faithful deliverer, the record continued, the mercy that answered, vv.11); the further oppressors recalled (the record, Nehemiah 9:28) — the oppressors (Zidonians, Amalekites); 'ye cried to me, and I delivered you out of their hand' (God delivering at every cry, the faithful mercy, the figure, the God who has always answered the cry of his people, the providence, the record, God's poignant refusal to follow, vv.13) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: The Zidonians also, and the Amalekites, and the Maonites, did oppress you; and ye cried to me, and I delivered you out of their hand

schools.summary: God's solemn refusal, that because Israel had forsaken him for other gods he would deliver them no more: 'yet ye have forsaken me, and served other gods: wherefore I will deliver you no more' — the just refusal — 'yet ye have forsaken me, and served other gods: wherefore I will deliver you no more': God, having rehearsed his mercies, declares he will deliver them no more (the witnesses noting the significance — the righteous indignation of God against a people who, delivered again and again, had repaid every mercy with fresh apostasy, the solemn refusal designed not to abandon them utterly but to humble them, to bring home the guilt of their ingratitude and to test whether their cry was true repentance or mere desire for relief, the refusal a holy rebuke, God not wearied of mercy but resolved that they should feel the weight of their sin, the word that would break a false peace and search the heart, the lesson — that God will not be trifled with, his patience not to be presumed upon, the grace abused that turns at last to righteous refusal, 'because I have called, and ye refused... I also will laugh at your calamity,' Proverbs 1:24-26, the truth that a repentance born only of distress, that seeks deliverance but not the Deliverer, is not yet true, and God's refusal searches whether the heart will turn to him or only from its pain, 'they returned and inquired early after God... nevertheless they did flatter him,' Psalm 78:34-37, the figure — the holy severity that is the servant of mercy, the refusal meant to drive the soul from its idols to the only Saviour, fulfilled in the gospel's warning that grace despised is grace endangered, 'how shall we escape, if we neglect so great salvation?' Hebrews 2:3, the truth that God's seeming denial is often the means of a deeper turning, the figure of the just refusal of grace abused, the holy God who will not be mocked by a repentance born only of distress, the call to come to God in true repentance and not merely to escape our trouble, the warning that presumed-upon mercy may meet a righteous refusal, the figure of the just refusal of grace abused, the holy God who will not be mocked by a repentance born only of distress); God charging Israel with forsaking him and serving other gods despite all his deliverances, the ingratitude that had repaid every mercy with apostasy (the grace abused, Proverbs 1:24-26) — the charge (ye have forsaken me); 'wherefore I will deliver you no more' (God declaring he would deliver them no more, the holy rebuke meant to humble them and search whether their cry was true repentance, the grace abused that turns to righteous refusal, the seeming denial the servant of a deeper mercy, the refusal that searches the heart, Psalm 78:34-37; Hebrews 2:3) — the figure (the holy severity that is the servant of mercy, the providence, the refusal, Israel's true repentance to follow, vv.15-16) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does God's refusal 'I will deliver you no more' mean?

sides.position: The just refusal of grace abused, the holy God who will not be mocked by a repentance born only of distress - the righteous indignation against a people who repaid every mercy with apostasy, the refusal meant not to abandon but to humble and test whether their cry was true repentance (Proverbs 1:24-26), the repentance born of distress not yet true (Psalm 78:34-37), the holy severity that is the servant of mercy, driving the soul from idols to the Saviour (Hebrews 2:3)

the righteous indignation of God against a people who repaid every mercy with fresh apostasy, the refusal meant not to abandon but to humble, to test whether their cry was true repentance or mere desire for relief

schools.summary: God's biting irony, bidding Israel cry to the gods they had chosen and let those deliver them: 'Go and cry unto the gods which ye have chosen; let them deliver you in the time of your tribulation' — the exposing of idols — 'Go and cry unto the gods which ye have chosen; let them deliver you in the time of your tribulation': God ironically bids them seek help from the idols they preferred to him (the witnesses noting the significance — the cutting irony of God, who bids the apostates turn for deliverance to the very gods they had chosen in his stead, that they might feel the folly and shame of their idolatry, the idols they had served exposed as powerless to save in the hour of need, the choice of false gods over the true thrown back upon them, the irony not mockery for its own sake but a holy rebuke meant to drive them to despair of their idols and back to God, the gods of their choosing summoned to do what only God can do, the lesson — that the idols men choose are utterly unable to save, and that God exposes their impotence in the day of trouble, 'they have mouths, but they speak not... they that make them are like unto them,' Psalm 115:5-8, the truth that whatever a man puts in God's place will fail him in his extremity, the chosen idol dumb in the hour of need, 'can any of the vanities of the Gentiles cause rain?' Jeremiah 14:22, the figure — the bankruptcy of every false confidence, fulfilled in the day when all who trusted other saviours find them helpless, 'where are thy gods that thou hast made thee? let them arise, if they can save thee,' Jeremiah 2:28, the truth that the irony is mercy in disguise, meant to shame the soul out of its idols, the figure of the holy irony that exposes the impotence of idols and the folly of forsaking the living God, the call to forsake the idols that cannot save and return to the God who can, the warning that the gods we choose will fail us in the day of tribulation, the figure of the holy irony that exposes the impotence of idols and the folly of forsaking the living God); God bidding them cry to the gods they had chosen in his stead, the choice of idols thrown back upon them (the chosen idols, Psalm 115:5-8) — the irony (cry to your chosen gods); 'let them deliver you in the time of your tribulation' (God bidding the idols deliver them, exposing their utter impotence to save in the hour of need, the false confidence bankrupt, the chosen god dumb in the day of trouble, the idols that cannot save, Jeremiah 14:22; Jeremiah 2:28) — the figure (the bankruptcy of every false confidence, the providence, the irony, Israel's true repentance to follow, vv.15) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does God's irony 'cry unto the gods which ye have chosen' accomplish?

sides.position: The holy irony that exposes the impotence of idols and the folly of forsaking the living God - the cutting irony bidding the apostates seek deliverance from the idols they chose, that they feel the shame of their idolatry and find the idols powerless, a holy rebuke driving them back to God, the idols utterly unable to save (Psalm 115:5-8), whatever a man puts in God's place failing him (Jeremiah 14:22), the bankruptcy of every false confidence (Jeremiah 2:28)

the cutting irony of God, who bids the apostates turn for deliverance to the very gods they had chosen in his stead, that they might feel the folly and shame of their idolatry and find the idols powerless to save

the idols men choose utterly unable to save and exposed in the day of trouble, whatever a man puts in God's place failing him, the bankruptcy of every false confidence (Psalm 115:5-8; Jeremiah 14:22; Jeremiah 2:28)

schools.summary: Israel's true repentance, confessing their sin and submitting wholly to God's will while pleading only for deliverance: 'and the children of Israel said unto the LORD, We have sinned: do thou unto us whatsoever seemeth good unto thee; deliver us only, we pray thee, this day' — the unreserved submission of faith — 'and the children of Israel said unto the LORD, We have sinned: do thou unto us whatsoever seemeth good unto thee; deliver us only, we pray thee, this day': Israel confesses sin, submits to whatever God sees fit, and casts itself on his mercy (the witnesses noting the significance — the marks of a true and deepening repentance, no longer a mere cry for relief but a humble confession of sin, an entire submission to God's righteous will, 'do thou unto us whatsoever seemeth good,' and an appeal cast wholly upon his free mercy and not their own desert, 'deliver us only, we pray thee,' the soul that has felt the weight of God's refusal now bowing to his justice and throwing itself unreservedly on his grace, the repentance proved real by its submission, willing to bear God's chastening if only he will not forsake them, the heart that resigns itself to God yet clings to him for deliverance, the lesson — that true repentance submits to God's will and appeals only to his mercy, neither presuming on deliverance nor despairing of it, 'I will bear the indignation of the LORD, because I have sinned against him,' Micah 7:9, the truth that the broken and submissive heart is the one God will not despise, the soul that says 'do unto me as seemeth good' laying hold of mercy by its very submission, 'a broken and a contrite heart, O God, thou wilt not despise,' Psalm 51:17, the figure — the penitent who casts himself wholly on God, fulfilled in the prodigal who returns confessing and the publican who cries for mercy, 'God be merciful to me a sinner,' Luke 18:13, the truth that such submission is the very door through which mercy returns, the figure of the genuine repentance that casts itself unreservedly upon the mercy of God, the call to submit wholly to God and appeal only to his grace, the assurance that the contrite heart God will not despise, the figure of the genuine repentance that casts itself unreservedly upon the mercy of God); Israel confessing their sin and submitting to whatever God saw fit, the entire resignation to his righteous will, the heart bowing to his justice (the submission of faith, Micah 7:9) — the submission (whatsoever seemeth good); 'deliver us only, we pray thee, this day' (Israel casting itself wholly on God's free mercy, pleading only for deliverance and not on their desert, the broken and contrite heart God will not despise, the penitent who casts himself on God alone, the appeal to mercy, Psalm 51:17; Luke 18:13) — the figure (the penitent who casts himself wholly on God, the providence, the repentance, Israel putting away their gods to follow, vv.16) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What marks Israel's repentance here as true?

sides.position: The genuine repentance that casts itself unreservedly upon the mercy of God - confession of sin, entire submission to God's will ('do thou whatsoever seemeth good'), and appeal cast wholly on his free mercy, true repentance submitting to God's will and appealing only to mercy (Micah 7:9), the broken and contrite heart God will not despise (Psalm 51:17), the penitent who casts himself wholly on God (Luke 18:13)

themes: And the children of Israel said unto the LORD, We have sinned: do thou unto us whatsoever seemeth good unto thee; deliver us only, we pray thee, this day

Israel's true repentance, confessing their sin and submitting wholly to God's will while pleading only for deliverance (the crux of the genuine repentance that casts itself unreservedly upon God's mercy)

the marks of a true and deepening repentance, no longer a mere cry for relief but a humble confession, an entire submission to God's will, and an appeal cast wholly on his free mercy and not their own desert

true repentance submitting to God's will and appealing only to mercy, the broken and contrite heart God will not despise, the penitent who casts himself wholly on God (Micah 7:9; Psalm 51:17; Luke 18:13)

schools.summary: Israel putting away their idols and serving the LORD, and the LORD's soul being grieved for the misery of Israel: 'and they put away the strange gods from among them, and served the LORD: and his soul was grieved for the misery of Israel' — the compassion of God — 'and they put away the strange gods from among them, and served the LORD: and his soul was grieved for the misery of Israel': Israel proves its repentance by deeds, and God is moved with pity at their misery (the witnesses noting the significance — repentance made real by reformation, the putting away of the idols and the actual return to God's service, met by the tender compassion of God, whose 'soul was grieved' (literally, was shortened or impatient) at the misery of his people, the affecting picture of the Lord unable to bear the sight of their distress, his fatherly heart yearning over them the moment they turn, the true repentance proved by forsaking the idols, the divine compassion that cannot endure the misery of the penitent, the LORD moved as a father moved over a suffering child, the lesson — that God is full of tender mercy toward the truly penitent, his compassions failing not, his heart turned the moment his people turn, 'in all their affliction he was afflicted... in his love and in his pity he redeemed them,' Isaiah 63:9, the truth that the misery of his people moves the heart of God, who afflicts not willingly and is grieved at their grief, 'how shall I give thee up?... mine heart is turned within me, my repentings are kindled together,' Hosea 11:8, the figure — the father running to meet the returning prodigal, moved with compassion, fulfilled in the God whose mercy triumphs the moment the sinner turns, 'when he was yet a great way off, his father saw him, and had compassion,' Luke 15:20, the truth that God's grieving over his people's misery is the spring of their deliverance, the figure of the tender compassion of God, who is moved by the misery of his people and cannot long withhold his mercy from the penitent, the call to trust the tender heart of God toward the contrite, the assurance that God is grieved at our misery and swift to pity the penitent, the figure of the tender compassion of God, who is moved by the misery of his people and cannot long withhold his mercy from the penitent); Israel putting away their idols and actually returning to God's service, the true repentance proved by forsaking the idols and doing the deeds of reformation (the repentance made real, Isaiah 63:9) — the reformation (put away the strange gods); 'and his soul was grieved for the misery of Israel' (the LORD's soul grieved, shortened, impatient at the misery of his people, the tender compassion of God moved as a father over a suffering child, his heart turned the moment they turned, his mercy unable to endure their distress, the divine pity, Hosea 11:8; Luke 15:20) — the figure (the father running to meet the returning prodigal, moved with compassion, the providence, the gathering against Ammon and the rise of Jephthah to follow, vv.17-18; ch.11) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the LORD's soul being grieved for Israel's misery reveal?

sides.position: The tender compassion of God, who is moved by the misery of his people and cannot long withhold his mercy from the penitent - God's soul shortened and impatient at their distress, unable to bear the sight of their misery, his fatherly heart yearning the moment they turn, afflicted in their affliction (Isaiah 63:9), his heart turned and repentings kindled (Hosea 11:8), the father moved with compassion over the returning prodigal (Luke 15:20)

themes: And they put away the strange gods from among them, and served the LORD: and his soul was grieved for the misery of Israel
Israel putting away their idols and serving the LORD, and the LORD's soul grieved for the misery of Israel (the crux of the tender compassion of God moved by the misery of his people)
repentance made real by reformation, met by the tender compassion of God whose soul was shortened and impatient at the misery of his people, unable to bear the sight of their distress

God full of tender mercy toward the penitent, afflicted in their affliction, his heart turned the moment they turn, the father running to meet the returning prodigal (Isaiah 63:9; Hosea 11:8; Luke 15:20)

schools.summary: Ammon and Israel gathering for war in Gilead and Mizpeh: 'then the children of Ammon were gathered together, and encamped in Gilead. And the children of Israel assembled themselves together, and encamped in Mizpeh' — the two armies gathered for the coming war (the then the children of Ammon were gathered together, and encamped in Gilead, Ammon encamped, the and the children of Israel assembled themselves together, and encamped in Mizpeh, Israel encamped, the figure, the armies drawn up, the penitent people now facing the foe with hope of God's help, the truth that Israel, having repented, gathered to face Ammon, the deliverance near, Judges 11:29, the figure of the people who, having turned to God, now stand against the foe, the gathering of the armies, the muster for war, vv.16); Ammon encamping in Gilead (the muster, Judges 11:29) — Ammon (in Gilead); 'Israel assembled themselves together, and encamped in Mizpeh' (Israel encamping at Mizpeh, the muster, the figure, the people who, having turned to God, stand against the foe, the providence, the muster, the search for a captain and Jephthah to follow, vv.18; ch.11) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Then the children of Ammon were gathered together, and encamped in Gilead. And the children of Israel assembled themselves together, and encamped in Mizpeh
Ammon and Israel gathering for war in Gilead and Mizpeh
the two armies gathered for the coming war, Israel having repented now facing Ammon
with hope of God's help (Judges 11:29)

schools.summary: The people of Gilead seeking a captain to lead against Ammon: 'and the people and princes of Gilead said one to another, What man is he that will begin to fight against the children of Ammon? he shall be head over all the inhabitants of Gilead' — Gilead sought a leader, promising headship to whoever would fight Ammon (the and the people and princes of Gilead said one to another, What man is he that will begin to fight against the children of Ammon?, the search for a captain, the he shall be head over all the inhabitants of Gilead, the promise of headship, the figure, the people seeking a deliverer, the way prepared for Jephthah, the truth that Gilead's search for a captain prepared the way for Jephthah's rise, Judges 11:1, the figure of the providence that raises the deliverer through the people's need, the search for a captain, the way for Jephthah, vv.17); Gilead seeking a captain (the search, Judges 11:1) — the search (a captain); 'he shall be head over all the inhabitants of Gilead' (headship promised to the leader, the way for Jephthah, the figure, the providence that raises the deliverer through the people's need, the providence, the search, Jephthah's rise to follow, ch.11) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the people and princes of Gilead said one to another, What man is he that will begin to fight against the children of Ammon? he shall be head over all the inhabitants of Gilead
The people of Gilead seeking a captain to lead against Ammon
Gilead seeking a leader and promising headship to whoever would fight Ammon
the providence preparing the way for the rise of Jephthah (Judges 11:1)
